chunks.page_summary: This page describes the procedure for integrating Rheonics Smart Module Electronics' 4-20mA analog output signal with a LOGO! Siemens PLC for various Rheonics viscosity and density meters.
chunks.extracted_text:
Phone: +1 713 234 0863

Rheonics Sensor Output Analog signal (4-20mA) Integration with LOGO! Siemens PLC

Modified on: Tue, 1 Oct, 2024 at 12:02 AM

What products are involved?

Rheonics SRV viscometer (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-viscometer-srv/), SRD density-viscosity meter (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-density-meter-srd/), DVP gas density-viscosity meter (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-gas-lng-density-meter-dvp/), and DVM HPHT density-viscosity meter. (https://rheonics.com/products/hpht-pvt-density-meter-dvm/)

What is the purpose of this article?

This article describes the procedure for integrating Rheonics Smart Module Electronics (SME) 4-20mA signal into a LOGO! Siemens.

1. Overview

Rheonics viscosity and density meters bring fluid intelligence and process control to a broad range of customer applications. Each sensor is equipped with a Smart Module Electronics unit (SME), which drives the sensor, evaluates its response, and enables communication through all major communication protocols.

This article guides the correct wiring procedures for using SME's 4-20mA channels and reading the sensor measurements on a LOGO! Siemens uses its analog inputs (0-10V).
key_facts: The document describes the procedure for integrating Rheonics Smart Module Electronics (SME) 4-20mA analog signals with LOGO! Siemens PLCs.
The article mentions Rheonics SRV viscometer, SRD density-viscosity meter, DVP gas density-viscosity meter, and DVM HPHT density-viscosity meter as involved products.
Rheonics viscosity and density meters provide fluid intelligence and process control for various customer applications.
Each Rheonics sensor is equipped with a Smart Module Electronics (SME) unit.
The SME unit drives the sensor, evaluates its response, and enables communication through all major communication protocols.
The document was last modified on Tuesday, October 1, 2024, at 12:02 AM.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides instructions for getting started with Rheonics viscometers and density sensors, detailing required firmware, compatible systems, and the 4-20mA output channels of the Smart Module Electronics (SME).
chunks.extracted_text:
2. Getting Started
* Rheonics SRV (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-viscometer-srv/), SRD (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-density-meter-srd/), DVP (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-gas-lng-density-meter-dvp/), DVM (https://rheonics.com/products/hpht-pvt-density-meter-viscometer-dvm/) with minimum firmware version V03.20/0 or higher.
* LOGO! 24 CE, 12/24 RCE, 24CEo, 12/24 RCEo. These have 4 AI (0...10V)
* LOGO! Soft Comfort
* Windows 10, 64-bit
* 500 Ω Resistance

3. SME's 4-20mA Channels
The Rheonics Smart Module Electronics (SME) includes three in-built output channels for 4-20mA signals on the SME's housing, labeled CH1+, CH2+, and CH3+ (Figure 3). In this case, CH1+ will be used to get the viscosity output to the LOGO!

CH2+ and CH3+ can also be used for other measurements based on the configuration set in the RCP software and can be used depending on the application.

Figure 3. 4-20 mA channels on SME

For more information on the 4 -20 mA channels of the SME, take a look at Connecting4-20 mA outputs. (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297457-connecting-4-20-ma-outputs) It's crucial to make sure the sensor cables are wired properly as well: Connecting sensor cable colored wires to transmitter electronics (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297454-connecting-sensor-cable-colored-wires-to-transmitter-electronics)
key_facts: Rheonics SRV, SRD, DVP, and DVM products require a minimum firmware version of V03.20/0 or higher for operation.
LOGO! 24 CE, 12/24 RCE, 24CEo, and 12/24 RCEo devices feature 4 analog inputs with a range of 0 to 10 Volts.
The Rheonics Smart Module Electronics (SME) unit includes three integrated 4-20mA output channels.
Channel CH1+ on the SME is designated for transmitting viscosity output to the LOGO! system.
Channels CH2+ and CH3+ can be configured through the RCP software for various other measurements, depending on the application.
It is crucial to ensure that sensor cables are wired correctly to the transmitter electronics.
warnings_notes: CE (Compliance mark)
It's crucial to make sure the sensor cables are wired properly.
Swiss made (Origin note)

4. LOGO! Analog input wiring
Wire the SME's 4-20 mA output channel to one of LOGO!'s analog input terminals as shown in Figure 4. Since the LOGO! PLC uses 0-10V as input, a 500Ω resistor is required to convert the current signal into voltage. It's important to ensure the resistor is correctly installed for an accurate reading of the 4-20 mA signal.

Figure 4. LOGO! and SME wiring
Figure 4 features a 492Ω resistor, as well as connections on M and I2 for the analog input. For LOGO! 12/24 RCE, the analog inputs are I1, I2, I7 and I8. Any of them can be used.
Notice how the resistance has a margin of error and is not exactly 500Ω as expected. It is recommended to use a high-precision resistor to minimize the impact.
5. Rheonics Control Panel (RC) Settings
The 4-20mA settings panel, in the “Service” Tab, allows the user to read and set the upper and lower limits for each channel, as well as to set which parameter is assigned to each of them.
In Figure 5, viscosity is set on Channel 1 with an Upper Range Value (URV) of 3,000 cP, density on Channel 2, and temperature on Channel 3.
key_facts: The Rheonics SME's 4-20 mA output channel is wired to one of the LOGO! PLC's analog input terminals.
A resistor is necessary to convert the 4-20 mA current signal from the SME into a 0-10V voltage signal, which the LOGO! PLC uses as input.
The LOGO! 12/24 RCE PLC has analog input terminals I1, I2, I7, and I8, any of which can be used.
The 4-20mA settings panel within the Rheonics Control Panel's 'Service' Tab allows users to configure upper and lower limits for each channel and assign specific parameters.
Viscosity, density, and temperature can be assigned to different output channels (e.g., Channel 1 for viscosity, Channel 2 for density, Channel 3 for temperature).
warnings_notes: It's important to ensure the resistor is correctly installed for an accurate reading of the 4-20 mA signal.
The resistance shown in Figure 4 (492Ω) has a margin of error and is not exactly 500Ω as expected. It is recommended to use a high-precision resistor to minimize the impact.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides instructions on how to configure I/O settings and read analog input values within the LOGO! Soft Comfort software, specifically focusing on enabling analog inputs and understanding their scaling.
chunks.extracted_text:
6.2 IO Configuration
After the device has been added, the next step is to navigate to "I/O settings", located on the left-hand side of the navigation bar, to enable the analog input.

[Screenshot: LOGO!Soft Comfort software interface showing I/O settings]

Figure 7. LOGO! I/O Settings

There are three options available:
*   Enable 0 Als
*   Enable 2 Als (Input terminals I7 and I8)
*   Enable 4 Als (Input terminals I7, I8, I1 and I2)

Since the I2 input has been wired, it is necessary to enable all four analog inputs (AIs).

6.3 Reading the Value

LOGO! reads in the electric signal and, with further processing, converts it into a standardized value within the range 0 to 1000 (10-bit resolution). This new value is then used in the circuit as the input of other analog functions or blocks for further processing or logic, such as the Analog Amplifier which will be used in further steps (Figure 13). For example, a 0-10 V signal at 5 V is automatically scaled to 500, but to do it, an Analog Input Block is required first (Figure 8).

The Analog Input block can be seen and selected in the left tools bar of LOGO! Soft Comfort, under Instructions, Analog. This is all it takes to read an analog value; however, an Analog Flag is also necessary to avoid software complaints about an open connector.

[Screenshot: LOGO!Soft Comfort software interface showing Analog input block selection and a simple circuit diagram]

Figure 8. Analog Input Block
key_facts: To enable analog input after adding a device, navigate to "I/O settings" in the LOGO! Soft Comfort navigation bar.
There are three options for enabling Analog Inputs (AIs): 0, 2 (I7 and I8 terminals), or 4 (I7, I8, I1, and I2 terminals).
If the I2 input is wired, all four analog inputs (AIs) must be enabled.
LOGO! software converts electric signals into a standardized value ranging from 0 to 1000 with 10-bit resolution.
An Analog Input Block is required to read an analog value, and an Analog Flag is necessary to prevent software complaints about an open connector.
AI1 and AI2 correspond to input terminals I7 and I8, while AI3 and AI4 correspond to input terminals I1 and I2.
diagram_labels: Behavior of analog outputs in STOP mode
All outputs keep the last value
If 4 Als are supported on LOGO!, do you want to enable 2 Als or 4 Als?
To ensure compatibility with older devices, enable 2Als.
No Als are available for your circuit program.
Only AI1 and AI2 corresponding to input terminals I7 and I8 can be used in your circuit program.
AI1 and AI2 corresponding to input terminals I7 and I8 are available for use in your circuit program. Additionally, AI3 and AI4 corresponding to input terminals I1 and I2 are available for use.
warnings_notes: To ensure compatibility with older devices, enable 2Als.
An Analog Flag is also necessary to avoid software complaints about an open connector.

chunks.page_summary: This page details the simulation, scaling, and processing of analog signals for viscosity measurements using Rheonics equipment and LOGO! Soft Comfort software.
chunks.extracted_text:
To test the signal from the SME, the Service Tab on the RCP can be used to simulate a 10 mA value. By clicking on Test: 10 mA, the SME will output 10 mA to the LOGO!
Since the actual resistance value of the resistor in the analog input (Figure 4) is 492Ω, a voltage of around 4.92V is expected.

Figure 12. 10mA simulation on RCP

Figure 13 shows the 0-10 V analog input scaled to 491 automatically by LOGO! Analog Input Block AI4, which is mapped to I2, was used to read the signal on the physical input (see Figure 7 to read how the inputs are mapped).

ViscositySignal
AI4
AI
AM1
AM
491 AI4
491.0 AM1

Figure 13. Viscosity Signal on LOGO! Soft Comfort

To summarize, the SME outputs a current between 4 and 20 mA for viscosity values ranging from 0 to 3000 cP as configured in the RCP. By passing this current through a 500 Ω resistor, the corresponding voltage is picked up by the LOGO! in its analog input. This voltage is then scaled by the LOGO! to a range of 0 to 1000.

6.6 Scaling back the Analog Input

Depending on the application, knowing the actual viscosity value in cP may be necessary. To achieve this, the 0 - 1000 input value is multiplied by a defined gain, and an offset is added to the product, as shown in the following steps. For the calculations, the Analog Amplifier block can be used (Figure 14).
key_facts: The Service Tab on the RCP can simulate a 10 mA output from the SME.
A 492Ω resistor in the analog input is expected to produce a voltage of approximately 4.92V for a 10mA current.
The 0-10 V analog input is automatically scaled to 491 by LOGO! Analog Input Block AI4.
The SME outputs a current from 4 to 20 mA for viscosity values between 0 and 3000 cP.
A 500 Ω resistor converts the SME's current output into a voltage readable by the LOGO!'s analog input.
The LOGO! scales the analog input voltage to a range of 0 to 1000.
To obtain the actual viscosity value in cP, the scaled 0-1000 input value needs to be multiplied by a gain and have an offset added, using the Analog Amplifier block.
diagram_labels: Figure 13. Viscosity Signal on LOGO! Soft Comfort

chunks.page_summary: This page describes the functionality and configurable parameters of an Analog Amplifier, detailing its input/output signals and a linear equation for calculating viscosity based on a LOGO-scaled input.
chunks.extracted_text:
Analog amplifier
Ax A -> A AQ
Par -<

Short description
This SFB amplifies an analog input value and returns it at the analog output.

Connections	Description
Input Ax	Input Ax is one of the following analog signals:
	* AI1 to AI8 (*)
	* AM1 to AM6 (if 0BA6), AM1 to AM16 (if 0BA7), or AM1 to AM64 (if 0BA8)
	* NAI1 to NAI32 (if 0BA7 or 0BA8)
	* AQ1 and AQ2 (if 0BA7), or AQ1 to AQ8 (if 0BA8)
	* NAQ1 to NAQ16 (if 0BA7 or 0BA8)
	* The block number of a function with analog output

Parameter	Gain
		Range of values: -10.00 to 10.00
	Offset
		Range of values: -10000 to 10000
		p: Number of decimals
		Possible settings: 0, 1, 2, 3

Output AQ	Value range for AQ: -32768 to +32767

* AI1 to AI8: 0 to 10 V corresponds with 0 to 1000 (internal value).

Figure 14. Analog Amplifier

The scaling needed to calculate the viscosity follows a linear equation y = mx + b. Where “Y” is the viscosity in cP and “X” is the value between 0 and 1000 scaled by the LOGO. Take a look at Figure 15.

Viscosity vs LOGO Units

Figure 15. Viscosity vs Units
key_facts: The Analog Amplifier (SFB) amplifies an analog input value and outputs an analog value.
Input Ax of the amplifier can be configured to accept various analog signals including AI, AM, NAI, AQ, and NAQ types, or a block number of a function with analog output.
The Analog Amplifier allows configuration of Gain (range -10.00 to 10.00), Offset (range -10000 to 10000), and the number of decimals (p) for its parameters.
The output AQ has a value range from -32768 to +32767.
For AI1 to AI8 inputs, a 0 to 10 V signal corresponds to an internal value range of 0 to 1000.
Viscosity is calculated using a linear equation y = mx + b, where 'Y' represents viscosity in cP and 'X' represents a value between 0 and 1000, scaled by the LOGO.
warnings_notes: The asterisk (*) next to AI1 to AI8 indicates a special note.
AI1 to AI8: 0 to 10 V corresponds with 0 to 1000 (internal value).
Specific analog input signal types (AM, NAI, AQ, NAQ) are conditional on hardware versions or configurations, such as 'if 0BA6', 'if 0BA7', or 'if 0BA8'.

chunks.page_summary: This page details the methodology for calculating gain and offset for an analog amplifier used in viscosity measurements, providing specific points and formulas for the calculations.
chunks.extracted_text:
Two points are known:
* Point 1: At 4 mA (2V minimum signal), the reading is scaled to 200, corresponding to 0 cP. This is represented by the point (200, 0) on the graph.
* Point 2: At 20 mA (10 V maximum signal), the reading is scaled to 1000 units, corresponding to 3000 cP. This is shown by the point (1000, 3000) on the graph.

6.6.1 Gain Calculation for the Analog Amplifier

For the 4-20 mA signal, the minimum value (4 mA) corresponds to 2V (4 mA x 500Ω), which is scaled to 200 by the LOGO! leaving the remaining 800 units available to measure the entire viscosity range.
Gain = 3000cP / 800unit = 3.75 cP/unit

Another way to calculate it is by solving the slope from the linear equation in Figure 14.
Gain = (y2 - y1) / (x2 - x1) = (3000 - 0) / (1000 - 200) = 3.75 cP/unit

A gain of 3.75 cP/unit means that the smallest detectable change in viscosity is 3.75 cP; this is due to LOGO!'s 10-bit resolution. While this level of resolution may be enough for some applications, if more accurate measurements are needed a lower viscosity range must be set using RCP.

6.6.2 Offset Calculation for the Analog Amplifier

The offset can be calculated using the linear equation from Figure 15.
b = y - m * x = 0 - 3.75 * 200 = -750

The Analog Amplifier configuration is shown in Figure 16 with the calculated gain and offset.
key_facts: The 4-20 mA signal range is scaled, with 4 mA corresponding to 200 units and 0 cP, and 20 mA corresponding to 1000 units and 3000 cP.
The LOGO! PLC scales the 4-20 mA signal to 200-1000 units, providing 800 units for viscosity measurement.
The gain for the analog amplifier is calculated as 3.75 cP/unit, representing the smallest detectable change in viscosity due to the LOGO!'s 10-bit resolution.
The offset for the analog amplifier is calculated as -750 using the linear equation b = y - m * x.
If more accurate viscosity measurements are required, a lower viscosity range must be set using RCP.

The analog amplifier's output, configured with specific offset and gain values, indicates a scaled viscosity.
A 10 mA signal from the analog amplifier corresponds to a scaled viscosity of 1,088 cP.
The scaled viscosity value can be utilized for advanced control, setting specific setpoints, or configuring alarm limits.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides an analysis of analog versus digital communication for industrial sensors, detailing the pros and cons of each, and recommending digital communication for reliable viscosity control with Rheonics sensors.
chunks.extracted_text:
7. Analog vs. Digital - Pros and Cons

Analog communication, particularly 4-20 mA current signals, has been a longstanding standard in industrial settings. An analog signal's primary advantage is its simplicity. This simplicity means it requires very little configuration, and analog components are generally less expensive, which makes for a great option for basic measurements. However, analog communication comes with limitations:

*   Only one piece of information can be transmitted per channel, which makes it unsuitable for transferring diagnostic data or multiple measurements at the same time.
*   Analog signals are also more prone to electrical interference, which can degrade signal quality in electrically noisy industrial environments.
*   Over time, analog systems may need calibration to maintain accuracy, adding to maintenance efforts and potential downtime.

Many of the limitations of analog systems can be addressed by digital communication protocols like Modbus TCP (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/folders/81000217062) and Modbus RTU (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/folders/81000217005) which come by default on all Rheonics sensors:

*   Transmits multiple data types over a single communication interface at once. This way, measurements, device status, diagnostic information, and even control commands can be exchanged between devices and control systems.
*   Digital signals are inherently less susceptible to electrical noise, which makes them ideal for industrial settings with high electromagnetic interference. This is crucial for maintaining accurate and consistent data transmission and reliable process control.
*   Digital networks are scalable and flexible, allowing multiple devices to communicate over a single network cable or wireless connection

The choice between analog and digital communication ultimately depends on the application, however, for a reliable viscosity control, it is heavily recommended the use of digital communication. For detailed information on all available communication options for Rheonics viscosity and density meters, visit Communication: Rheonics Support (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/81000100945).
key_facts: Analog communication (4-20 mA current signals) is a longstanding industrial standard, offering simplicity, low configuration requirements, and generally less expensive components for basic measurements.
Limitations of analog communication include transmitting only one piece of information per channel, susceptibility to electrical interference, and potential need for calibration over time.
Digital communication protocols like Modbus TCP and Modbus RTU are default on all Rheonics sensors and address analog system limitations.
Digital communication can transmit multiple data types over a single interface, including measurements, device status, diagnostic information, and control commands.
Digital signals are less susceptible to electrical noise and electromagnetic interference, which ensures accurate and consistent data transmission.
Digital networks are scalable and flexible, supporting communication between multiple devices via cable or wireless connections.
For reliable viscosity control, Rheonics heavily recommends the use of digital communication, although the ultimate choice depends on the application.
warnings_notes: For reliable viscosity control, the use of digital communication is heavily recommended.
